question_title: What will be the maximum intraday value of the VIX over these biweekly periods in Q1 2026? (Mar 16 - Mar 27)
summary: Forecasting the maximum intraday VIX high over Mar 16-27, 2026 (10 trading days). The VIX is currently in an elevated regime (close 20.49, regime mean ~19.2) driven by tariff policy uncertainty (Supreme Court struck down IEEPA tariffs Feb 20, new Section 122 tariffs imposed Feb 24). A major catalyst — the FOMC meeting on Mar 17-18 — falls within the target period. Recent biweekly max highs have been 21.0-23.1 in a slightly lower VIX regime; the current higher baseline and catalysts push the expected max higher. Monte Carlo simulations (OU process with jumps) and scenario-mixture models both converge on a median around 26, with significant right-skew reflecting VIX's jump risk. The 10-day gap before the period introduces uncertainty about the starting VIX level due to mean-reversion from today's 25.24 spike.

factors.description: Current elevated VIX regime: VIX closed at 20.49 on Mar 2 with intraday high of 25.24, regime mean ~19.2. Starting from elevated baseline increases the floor for max-high.
FOMC meeting Mar 17-18 falls inside the target period. FOMC announcements are known VIX catalysts that widen intraday ranges and can produce 2-5 point VIX spikes.

Tariff policy uncertainty: Supreme Court struck down IEEPA tariffs Feb 20, Trump imposed new 10% Section 122 tariff Feb 24. Ongoing trade tension creates persistent background volatility and risk of sudden escalation spikes.
Empirical recent biweekly max highs: 20.99, 23.10, 22.96 in Jan-Feb when VIX averaged 17-20. Current higher baseline suggests Mar 16-27 max high will likely exceed these.

Mean-reversion tendency: VIX spikes typically mean-revert within 10 days (empirical median forward return -4.85% after comparable spikes). By Mar 16, VIX may have partially reverted from today's spike, lowering the starting baseline.
VIX max-high is right-skewed by construction: over 10 trading days, the maximum of daily highs has heavy right tail. Monte Carlo simulations consistently show P90 values 1.5-2x the median.

reasoning:
**Forecast Summary: Maximum Intraday VIX (Mar 16-27, 2026)**

| Percentile | Value |
|-----------|-------|
| P10 | 20.5 |
| P20 | 22.5 |
| P40 | 25.0 |
| P60 | 28.0 |
| P80 | 33.0 |
| P90 | 38.0 |

**Key drivers:**
- **Elevated baseline**: VIX at 20.49 with regime mean ~19.2, driven by tariff uncertainty
- **FOMC catalyst**: March 17-18 meeting falls right at the start of the period
- **Tariff backdrop**: Supreme Court struck down IEEPA tariffs, new Section 122 tariffs imposed — policy remains volatile
- **Right-skew**: VIX max-high distributions have heavy right tails; a single spike day can dominate

The median estimate of ~26-27 reflects an elevated-but-not-crisis scenario where the FOMC meeting and tariff noise produce at least one spike day above the baseline close level. The wide P10-P90 range (20.5 to 38.0) reflects genuine uncertainty about whether conditions calm or escalate further.

resolution_criteria:
This question is a subquestion of a group question. This subquestion specifically pertains to (and resolves according to) the option 'Mar 16 - Mar 27'. The resolution criteria for the parent question is below. 

Each subquestion will resolve as the maximum intraday value of the VIX recorded from the market open on the first Monday to the market close on the last Friday of the specified biweekly period. Specifically, it will resolve as the highest HIGH value for the relevant days in the CSV published [on the Cboe website](https://www.cboe.com/tradable_products/vix/vix_historical_data/).
fine_print:
* If the market is closed on one or more trading days within the period, only the available trading days will be considered.
* If the resolution source delays or ceases its reporting of these data, Metaculus might use alternative credible sources to resolve this question.

background_info:
The [CBOE Volatility Index (VIX)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/VIX), often referred to as the “fear gauge,” is a real-time market index that represents the market’s expectations for volatility over the coming 30 days. It is derived from the prices of S\&P 500 index options and is widely viewed as a measure of market risk and investor sentiment.

The VIX tends to spike during periods of high uncertainty or panic, often driven by earnings reports, economic data releases, Federal Reserve announcements, or geopolitical events. Predicting the maximum intraday value can provide insights into how market participants perceive risk over a short horizon.

Historical values for the VIX are available from TradingView [here](https://www.tradingview.com/symbols/CBOE-VIX/).
